quick_facts.label: What the scheme is
quick_facts.value: The JAG Entry Scheme is an Indian Army Short Service Commission (Non-Technical) entry for law graduates into the Judge Advocate General Branch; successful trainees are commissioned as Lieutenants.

quick_facts.label: Current recruitment context
quick_facts.value: For the 125th JAG Course, scheduled to commence in April 2027, the Indian Army notification reports 10 combined vacancies for unmarried male and female law graduates. Vacancy numbers, course dates and application windows are notification-specific and can change in later intakes.

quick_facts.label: Core academic eligibility
quick_facts.value: The current 125th-course notification requires at least 55% aggregate in a three-year LL.B. after graduation or a five-year integrated LL.B.; the degree institution must be recognised by the Bar Council of India and the applicant must be eligible to register as an advocate with a State Bar Council/Bar Council of India.

quick_facts.label: CLAT PG requirement
quick_facts.value: For the 125th course, CLAT PG 2026 is mandatory, including for LL.M.-qualified or LL.M.-appearing candidates; the score is used for shortlisting and must be verified at the Selection Centre before SSB.

quick_facts.label: Selection and training
quick_facts.value: The notified route is online application, shortlisting using CLAT PG score, SSB interview, medical examination, merit and pre-commission training at Officers Training Academy (OTA), Chennai. The preceding 124th-course notification specifies 49 weeks of OTA training, illustrating that training details should be checked against the intake-specific notification.

quick_facts.label: Legal function
quick_facts.value: Under the Army Rules, a judge-advocate represents the Judge-Advocate General at a court-martial, advises on legal questions and procedural defects, sums up evidence and its legal bearing, and must remain impartial.

sections.heading: What a JAG officer does
paragraphs: A JAG officer is a commissioned Army legal officer, not a civilian legal adviser. The statutory court-martial role includes representing the Judge-Advocate General, giving legal opinions to the prosecutor and accused on questions relating to the charge or trial, identifying irregularities in charges or the constitution of the court, advising the court and convening officer, and summing up the evidence and its legal bearing before deliberation.

The role requires procedural fairness as well as legal analysis. The Army Rules state that a judge-advocate shares responsibility for ensuring that an accused person is not disadvantaged by their position or inability to examine or cross-examine witnesses, and requires the judge-advocate to maintain an entirely impartial position. Court-martial proceedings must also be recorded accurately.

The Army Act provides that every general court-martial, and potentially district or summary general courts-martial, may be attended by a judge-advocate from the Judge Advocate General’s Department or another officer approved by the Judge Advocate General.
bullets: Advise on military-law questions arising from charges and trials.
Identify procedural or legal defects and advise the court and convening authority.
Assist in safeguarding fairness for the accused while maintaining impartiality.
Record or ensure accurate recording of court-martial proceedings where acting as judge-advocate.

sections.heading: Entry route from law school
paragraphs: The direct route represented by this node is the Indian Army’s intake-specific JAG Entry Scheme notification. Applicants apply online through Join Indian Army when the relevant JAG Short Service Commission course is open. The 125th-course notification, for an April 2027 course, states that the entry is open to unmarried male and unmarried female law graduates and that applications are made online.

Applicants should treat eligibility as intake-specific. For the 125th course, the stated age band is 21–27 years as of January 1, 2027; it requires a minimum 55% aggregate LL.B., eligibility for advocate registration, a Bar Council of India-recognised college or university, and CLAT PG 2026. An LL.M. does not replace the specified CLAT PG requirement for that notification.

The notified selection sequence is application and CLAT PG-based shortlisting, SSB selection, medical examination, merit list and issue of joining instructions. Passing SSB alone does not guarantee joining: final selection remains subject to merit, medical fitness, eligibility and the notified number of vacancies.
bullets: Complete an eligible three-year LL.B. or five-year integrated LL.B. and retain degree/marks documentation.
Take the CLAT PG examination specified by the relevant Army JAG notification.
Apply through the Join Indian Army officer-entry portal during the published window.
If shortlisted, complete SSB, medical examination and document/CLAT-score verification.
Join OTA, Chennai only if placed on final merit within the available vacancies.

sections.heading: Skills indicated by the legal role
paragraphs: The Army Rules make strong legal reasoning and military-procedure knowledge central to the role: a judge-advocate must advise on questions of law, evaluate legal and procedural irregularities, explain the legal bearing of evidence and guide the court on points of law. The Rules also emphasise knowledge of general legal principles, rules of evidence and courts-martial practice and procedure.

Equally important are careful written-record practices, impartial judgement and the ability to support fair proceedings. These are role requirements grounded in the governing Rules rather than a separate employability checklist.
bullets: Legal research, statutory interpretation and evidence analysis.
Knowledge of courts-martial practice, procedure and military law.
Clear, impartial legal advice under formal process requirements.
Accurate drafting and record-management discipline.
Professional judgement when dealing with procedural fairness.

sections.heading: Work setting and training environment
paragraphs: JAG officers serve as Army officers in the Judge Advocate General Branch. Their formal legal work can involve courts-martial and related military-justice processes, where the Army Rules prescribe advisory, procedural and record-related functions for a judge-advocate.

Selected JAG candidates undergo pre-commission training at Officers Training Academy, Chennai. The 124th JAG-course notification specified a 49-week course and stated that successful cadets receive a Post Graduate Diploma in Defence Management and Strategic Studies from the University of Madras; candidates should verify the duration and award in the notification for their own intake.
bullets: Military legal and justice settings, including court-martial-related duties.
Officer pre-commission training at OTA, Chennai.
Service conditions, medical standards and postings are governed by Army policies and the applicable recruitment notification.

sections.heading: Commission, tenure and progression
paragraphs: On successful completion of training, the JAG-entry candidate is commissioned as a Lieutenant. The 124th-course notice lists time-based promotion milestones of Captain after two years of reckonable commissioned service, Major after six years and Lieutenant Colonel after 13 years; selection-based progression beyond this is subject to the applicable service rules and conditions.

JAG entry is a Short Service Commission route. The 124th-course notification describes an initial 10-year tenure that may be extended by four years. It also says eligible and suitable officers may be considered for Permanent Commission in their 10th year under policies in force at that time; this is consideration, not an automatic entitlement. Terms can be amended, so applicants should rely on the notification and policy applicable to their own course.
bullets: Commissioned rank on passing out: Lieutenant.
Published time-based milestones in the 124th-course notice: Captain at 2 years, Major at 6 years and Lieutenant Colonel at 13 years of reckonable commissioned service.

Short Service Commission terms in that notice: 10 years initially, potentially extendable to 14 years; consideration for Permanent Commission may arise in the 10th year subject to eligibility, suitability and policy.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: Compensation is government pay-matrix based and should be treated as dated, variable context rather than a guaranteed take-home amount. The 124th-course notification lists a cadet stipend of ₹56,100 per month during OTA training. On commissioning, it lists Lieutenant pay at Level 10, ₹56,100–₹177,500, and fixed Military Service Pay of ₹15,500 per month for ranks from Lieutenant through Brigadier.

The same notice describes additional allowances as conditional on the applicable rules, rank and posting area. It also presents an approximate annual CTC figure; that estimate is not a salary guarantee because allowances, deductions, posting and government pay revisions can change actual compensation.
bullets: Training stipend stated in the 2026 124th-course notice: ₹56,100 per month.
Lieutenant pay matrix stated in that notice: Level 10, ₹56,100–₹177,500.
Military Service Pay stated in that notice: ₹15,500 per month for Lieutenant to Brigadier.
Allowances and total compensation vary by entitlement, location, rank and rules in force.
